preFix.title: Needle-felted badger
preFix.subtitle: A 12 cm sitting badger in black, white, and grey merino, with the characteristic striped head
preFix.excerpt: A sitting needle-felted badger in grey body with a white face and two black stripes running from snout to ears. Black legs and rump. Head is separately felted and attached. The head stripes are worked in as inlay colour, not applied on top.
preFix.sourceNotes: Needle-felted UK woodland animals are a standard project in British fibre arts teaching. Badger anatomy and colour-stripe construction synthesised from general needle felting practice; not drawn from a specific PD source.

content.text: A badger is an ideal beginner needle felting animal because its distinctive markings are bold enough to be forgiving: the strong black-and-white contrast on the face reads clearly even if the needle work is not perfectly smooth. The body is grey
roving
in a
sitting pose
, approximately 12 cm tall. The head is felted separately and attached. The face starts white and the two black stripes are worked in using the
inlay
technique with a fine
felting needle
. Two small stubby front legs are added to the sitting torso. The sitting pose means no complex back-leg construction is required.

attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. The needle must enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.
attrs.title: Needle safety

items.name: Felting needle, 40-gauge (for inlay and surface finishing, optional)
Foam felting mat, at least 10 cm thick

content.text: Method
Step 1: Form the body

Roll a large handful of grey roving into an egg-shaped mass wider at the haunch end and narrower at the top. In the sitting pose, the haunch end sits on the ground and the top tapers to a neck. Needle the mass on the foam mat with the 36-gauge needle, rotating constantly, until it is firm and measures approximately 8 cm tall and 7 cm wide at the haunches. The underside should be flat so the figure sits without rocking.
Step 2: Add the black rump and legs

Lay thin wisps of black roving over the lower two-thirds of the body (the haunch and rump area) and needle in using the inlay technique. Work in multiple thin layers rather than one thick layer. For the two small front legs, roll short cylinders of black roving (each about 3 cm long, 1.5 cm in diameter) and needle until firm. Attach to the lower front of the body.
Step 3: Form the head

Roll white roving into a rounded wedge shape about 6 cm long and 4 cm wide. The badger head is wider at the cheeks and narrower at the snout. Needle on the mat with the 38-gauge needle until firm but not yet finished. The head will be entirely white at this stage; colour is added in the next step. Do not attach to the body yet.
Step 4: Work the black stripes as inlay

Lay a very thin wisp of black roving on one side of the white head, running from the snout area up and back toward where the ear will be. Needle the black wisp in gently with the 40-gauge needle (or 38-gauge if that is what you have), using shallow strokes that embed the black into the white surface without pushing it all the way through. The stripe should appear as a bold band of black on the white face, approximately 1 cm wide. Repeat for the second stripe on the other side. Both stripes should be symmetrical.
Step 5: Add the nose and ears

Add a small dome of black roving at the very tip of the snout and needle in. For each ear, needle a small rounded triangle of grey roving on the mat (about 2 cm × 1.5 cm), leaving the base edge loose. Attach the ears at the top of the head, one on each side, positioned at the back of the stripes.
Step 6: Attach the head and finish

Hold the completed head against the top of the body and needle through the neck join from all angles. Add a small roll of grey roving around the neck if a gap is visible, and needle it in to blend the join. Work over the full figure with light strokes from the 38-gauge needle to smooth any loose surface fibres. Check the stripe symmetry and needle in any stray black or white fibres.

items.fix: For inlay colour work, use a 40-gauge needle and only shallow strokes (5-8 mm deep). If stripes have blurred, add a fresh thin wisp of black on top and needle in very shallowly.
items.cause: Needling was too deep, pushing black fibres through the white layer and mixing them
items.problem: The black stripes have blurred into the white and look grey

items.fix: Before starting the second stripe, hold the head at arm's length and mark the midline of the face. Position the second stripe to mirror the first.
items.cause: The stripe position was not measured or checked before needling began
items.problem: The two stripes are not symmetrical

items.fix: Place the figure on the foam mat, haunch-side down, and needle the underside with the 36-gauge needle until it is flat enough to sit level.
items.cause: The haunch end of the body was not needled flat during Step 1
items.problem: The figure rocks and will not sit flat

content.text: Care and finishing

Spot-clean with a barely damp cloth. Keep away from moisture and direct sunlight. Display on a shelf or in a shadow box. The white areas of the face will show dust sooner than the darker parts; a soft dry brush removes loose surface dust without disturbing the inlay colour work.
